pages.titleString: Will this primarily be printed, or read on a screen?
pages.helpString: Pick the way most people will use this. You can change anything later.

options.label: Primarily printed on paper
options.description: A book or booklet that will be printed.

options.label: Read on a screen
options.description: A PDF for a phone, tablet, or computer.
cons: Some people cannot read on screens.

pages.titleString: Is this a finished version, or a draft?
pages.helpString: Drafts and finished versions have different settings.

questions.label: Publication stage
questions.translatorNote: Pre-publication vs public release. Affects product type options and sensitive flag.

options.label: A draft, during translation
options.description: A version for the team or community to read and check while translation is still going on.
pros: Shows project name and date by default.
Settings keep sensitive details visible for checking.

options.label: A finished version
options.description: A final version for general use.
cons: You will need to check translation quality before publishing.

pages.helpString: Pick up to 3. This helps put the most useful options at the top of the next list.
pages.staticPanel: The audience you choose is used to suggest a publication type. It does not hide any options — you can still pick any type you like.

questions.label: Audience
questions.translatorNote: Audience. Used to re-rank product types, not to hide them.

options.label: Children
options.description: Young readers, up to about age 12.

options.label: Young people
options.description: Teens and young adults.

options.label: Adults
options.description: General adult readers.

options.label: Church leaders
options.description: Pastors, teachers, and preachers.

options.label: Church members
options.description: People who attend church regularly.

options.label: New readers
options.description: People reading the Bible for the first time.

options.label: Low vision
options.description: Readers who need larger text or more space.

pages.titleString: What kind of book are you making?
pages.helpString: Each type comes with good starting settings. You can change anything later.

questions.label: Product type
questions.translatorNote: Product type. Options split by publicationStage.

options.label: Team check
options.description: A draft for the translation team to review.
pros: Good for sharing with the team.

options.label: Community check
options.description: A draft for the wider community, including comprehension testing.

options.label: Consultant / UNS check
options.description: A draft for a translation consultant or for back-translation checking.

options.label: Exegetical check
options.description: A draft focused on meaning, with notes and cross-references visible.
cons: More complex layout — not for general readers.

options.label: Pew Bible
options.description: A standard Bible for church use. Compact and affordable.
cons: Small text may be hard for older readers.

recommendIf.reason: A good all-round choice for church members.
A good all-round choice for adult readers.

options.label: Children's Bible
options.description: Larger text, pictures, simple layout.

recommendIf.reason: Designed for young readers.

options.label: Pastor's Bible
options.description: Smaller text, many cross-references and notes.

recommendIf.reason: Designed for church leaders who need cross-references and notes.

options.label: Large-print Bible
options.description: Big text and wide spacing for easier reading.
pros: Good for older readers or low-vision readers.
cons: More pages than a standard Bible.

recommendIf.reason: Designed for readers who need larger text.

options.label: Life Application Bible
options.description: Includes side notes and study material.
cons: Needs extra study content to be useful.

recommendIf.reason: Study notes help new readers understand context.

options.label: Journaling Bible
options.description: Wide outer margin with lines for writing.

options.label: Lectionary
options.description: A book of readings for church services.
pros: Large clear text for reading aloud.
cons: Only covers selected readings, not the full Bible.

recommendIf.reason: Good for churches that follow a lectionary cycle.

pages.staticPanel: Colour printing costs more, especially for inkjet and small print runs. Most Bibles and Scripture publications use black-and-white inside pages. Colour is useful for children's books or study materials with maps and diagrams.

questions.label: Colour
questions.translatorNote: Colour vs B&W. Affects PDF output format.

options.label: Black-and-white
options.description: Lower cost. Good for most Bibles.
cons: Cannot show colour pictures or maps.

options.label: Colour
options.description: Higher cost. Useful for children's books or study materials.
pros: Can include colour pictures, maps, and diagrams.

pages.titleString: What is the finished book size?
pages.helpString: This is the book page size, not paper size. (e.g. To print two-up A5 on A4 paper, choose A5.)

questions.label: Page size
questions.translatorNote: Page size selection. Order matches PTXprint Layout page UI.

options.label: A4  ·  210 × 297 mm  /  8.3 × 11.7 in
options.description: Standard printer paper worldwide.
pros: Easy to print at home or office.

options.label: A5 (Half A4)  ·  148 × 210 mm  /  5.8 × 8.3 in
options.description: The most common size for Bibles worldwide.
pros: Good balance of text space and portability.

options.label: Pocket A6  ·  105 × 148 mm  /  4.1 × 5.8 in
options.description: A small pocket-size format.
cons: Small text, limited space per page.

options.label: B5  ·  176 × 250 mm  /  6.9 × 9.8 in
options.description: Between A4 and A5. Good for study Bibles.

options.label: B6  ·  125 × 176 mm  /  4.9 × 6.9 in
options.description: Between A6 and A5 in size.

options.label: PA4  ·  210 × 280 mm  /  8.3 × 11.0 in
options.description: A4 width, slightly shorter than A4.
cons: Not a standard paper size in all countries.

options.label: PA5  ·  140 × 210 mm  /  5.5 × 8.3 in
options.description: A compact format close to A5.

options.label: Pocket (PA6)  ·  105 × 140 mm  /  4.1 × 5.5 in
options.description: A tiny pocket Bible size.

options.label: Letter  ·  216 × 279 mm  /  8.5 × 11 in
options.description: Standard US letter paper.
pros: Easy to print at home or office in North America.

options.label: Half Letter  ·  140 × 216 mm  /  5.5 × 8.5 in
options.description: Half of US Letter paper. Common in North America.
pros: Easy to print from US Letter paper.

options.label: Compact  ·  133 × 210 mm  /  5.25 × 8.25 in
options.description: A compact US-standard size.

options.label: Common  ·  147 × 221 mm  /  5.8 × 8.7 in
options.description: A popular trade paperback size.

options.label: KBS Max  ·  165 × 234 mm  /  6.5 × 9.2 in
options.description: Maximum size allowed by Korea Bible Society standards.

pages.titleString: About how many pages will the finished book have?
pages.helpString: An estimate is fine. This helps choose a binding.
pages.staticPanel: The number of pages affects which bindings are possible. It also affects spine width for hardcover and perfect-bound books.

questions.label: Page count
questions.translatorNote: Page count for binding gating and cover/spine calc.

pages.titleString: What kind of screen will people read this on?
pages.helpString: This sets the page size to match the screen.

questions.label: Screen target
questions.translatorNote: Digital target: phone/tablet/pc affects page size.

options.label: Phone
options.description: A small phone screen.
cons: Small screen — single column layout works better.

options.label: Tablet
options.description: A tablet or large phone screen.
cons: Fewer people have tablets than phones.

options.label: Computer
options.description: A desktop or laptop screen.
pros: Large screen with room for two columns.
cons: Fewer people in some communities use computers.

pages.helpString: The number of copies that fit each option is shown on the right.

questions.label: Printer
questions.translatorNote: Printer type for production overrides. Volume communicated via rangeLabel.

options.label: Home inkjet
options.description: A printer at home, for a few copies.

recommendIf.reason: These publications usually need many copies. A copy shop or print-on-demand service will be cheaper.
Home inkjet printers are slow for large books. Consider a copy shop or print-on-demand service.

options.label: High-speed office laser
options.description: A normal office printer.

recommendIf.reason: Most office laser printers print in black-and-white. Check yours supports colour before choosing this.
Office printers are not ideal for large page counts. Consider a copy shop or print-on-demand service.

options.label: Local copy shop
options.description: A nearby photocopy or print shop.
cons: You must deliver or send the file.

options.label: Print-on-demand (small runs)
options.description: Online services that print one or a few copies.
cons: Costs more per copy than offset.

options.label: Print-on-demand (bigger runs)
options.description: Online services with a minimum of around 50 copies.
pros: Lower cost per copy than small POD.

options.label: Offset printing
options.description: A commercial press for big print runs.
pros: Lowest cost per copy for large runs.
Wide choice of paper and binding.
cons: Needs at least 500 copies to be cost-effective.
Needs print-ready PDF with crop marks.

pages.helpString: The middle setting works well for most print runs.
pages.staticPanel: Moving left reduces file size and allows slightly uneven columns, which speeds up typesetting. Moving right produces more balanced columns and higher-resolution images, but takes longer to generate and produces a larger file.

questions.label: Budget vs quality
questions.translatorNote: Budget vs quality slider. Affects allowUnbalanced and viewer checks.

pages.helpString: The middle setting works well for most uses.
pages.staticPanel: Moving left produces the file faster with slightly simpler layout processing. Moving right takes more time but produces better-balanced text and higher-quality images.

questions.label: Speed vs quality
questions.translatorNote: Speed vs quality slider for digital output.

pages.titleString: How will the pages be held together?
pages.helpString: If you are not sure, choose Perfect-bound — it works for most books.

questions.label: Binding
questions.translatorNote: Binding type. Gated by pageCount and printer volumeBand.

options.label: Folded and stapled
options.description: Folded in the middle and stapled. Best for small books.
pros: Easy to print at home or a copy shop.
cons: Only works for small page counts.

recommendIf.reason: Works best under 48 pages. Your book may be hard to fold and staple at this thickness.
Folded-and-stapled binding does not work for books this thick.

options.label: Spiral or coil
options.description: A wire or plastic coil down the side. Lies flat when open.
pros: Easy to print at a copy shop.
cons: Not suitable for very thick books.

recommendIf.reason: Spiral binding can be done but gets awkward above 300 pages.
Spiral binding is not practical for books this thick.

options.label: Side-stitched
options.description: Stapled or sewn through the side.
pros: Works for thicker books than saddle-stitch.
cons: Does not lie flat when open.

recommendIf.reason: Side-stitching is not practical for books this thick.

options.label: Perfect-bound (paperback)
options.description: Pages glued at the spine, like a paperback.
pros: Works for a wide range of page counts.

recommendIf.reason: Can be done but the spine will be very thin. Saddle-stitch may be a better choice.
Perfect binding needs at least 32 pages.

options.label: Hardcover
options.description: A hard book cover with sewn or glued pages inside.
pros: Good for pew or reference use.

recommendIf.reason: Hardcover works but looks best on thicker books.
Hardcover binding needs at least 64 pages.
Hardcover binding requires a professional bindery — it cannot be done on a home or office printer.

questions.label: Special layout
questions.help: Leave all unticked if not sure. You can add these later.

options.label: Two languages, side by side
options.description: The text is shown in two languages, one beside the other.
pros: Readers can compare the two languages easily.

options.label: Interlinear scripture
options.description: Each word has another language under or above it.
pros: Useful for language learning or checking.
cons: Text is harder to read quickly.

questions.label: Columns
questions.help: Two columns fit more text on each page. One column is easier to read on a phone or for children.
questions.translatorNote: Column layout. Hidden when diglot is selected (diglot forces two columns).

options.label: One column
options.description: All text in a single block down the page.
pros: Easier to read for children, new readers, or on small screens.
cons: Fewer words per page — more pages needed.

options.label: Two columns
options.description: Two columns of text side by side.
pros: Shorter line length can improve readability.
cons: Narrower columns — not ideal for large text or small page sizes.
